behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: the code forgets to pass all taxonomy labels to the API function call. This is local to the module—no external dependencies required. We can mock the API client, verify the mock was called with all labels, and never need the real network. The only assumption is that the mocked API function signature matches reality, which is reasonable.
behaviors.test_description: Verify that when the module invokes the classification API function, the call includes all valid taxonomy labels (legal, technical, financial, other) in the request parameters. Mock the API client and assert the mock received all labels as arguments.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test stays at Unit level: single module boundary, mock API client, no I/O, no real dependencies.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns "unknown" instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: This is input validation—the module receives a response from the API and must filter it. No external infrastructure needed: mock the API to return an unexpected label, call the module, assert it returns 'unknown'. The failure (not filtering unexpected labels) is purely local logic.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API to return an out-of-vocabulary label (e.g., 'malware'). Call the module and verify it returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding 'malware' to the caller.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test stays at Unit: mock input, verify local validation logic, no integration or timing required.

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls.
behaviors.justification: If the module caches results in-memory, we can test this at Unit level: mock the API to return different labels on successive calls, call the module twice with the same document, and assert it returns the same label both times (proving it was cached, not calling the API twice). The failure (missing or broken cache) is revealed by the mock's call count. If caching were external (Redis), we'd need Integration, but the default assumption for 'classification result caching' is in-process memory.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the mocked API to return different labels on first and second call. Call the module twice with identical input. Verify the module returns the same label both times and the API mock was called exactly once (proving caching is working).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test stays at Unit if caching is in-process. If the actual implementation uses external caching, this would become Integration, but the test description and level assume in-process cache.

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: To verify token counting is accurate, we need a real token-counting mechanism—not a mock. Mocking token counts would always pass, hiding bugs like 'document is 50,000 tokens but code thinks it's 100'. We assume the module uses a local library (e.g., tiktoken for LLMs) rather than an external token-counting API. At Integration level, we use the real tokenizer library, mock the external API response, and verify the token count is within budget. This catches real token-counting bugs without requiring an external API call.
behaviors.test_description: Use a real tokenizer library (e.g., tiktoken) to count tokens in a sample document. Pass that document to the module with a token budget. Mock the external classification API response. Verify the module counted tokens accurately and did not exceed the budget. Test with both typical and edge-case documents (very short, very long).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires the real tokenizer library (Integration), not a mock. A Unit test with mocked token counts would give false negatives.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: the module doesn't retry, or retries incorrectly, or doesn't wait. At Unit level, we can mock the sleep function and the API, but then we're assuming the module's retry logic is wired correctly. At Integration level, we mock the sleep function (to avoid real delays) and mock the API to return a rate-limit error on the first call and success on the second, then verify the module eventually returned the result. The key is that we're testing the actual retry control flow (not mocking it away), and we mock sleep to keep the test fast. This catches bugs like missing sleep calls or retry loops that don't actually retry.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API to return a rate-limit error on first call and a valid classification on second call. Mock the sleep function to track calls. Call the module and verify: (1) sleep was called with the configured wait duration, (2) the module retried the API call, (3) the module returned the classification from the second call instead of raising an error to the caller.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test uses mocked sleep (to avoid delays) and mocked API (to control the error scenario), but tests the actual retry control flow. This is Integration, not Unit, because Unit testing with mocked sleep everywhere would miss bugs in the actual retry structure.
